title: Jokes or Gibberish? Humor Retention in Translation with Neural Machine Translation vs. Large Language Model
research_questions: How well do different translation models (MT, GPT, GPT-P, and GPT-Ex) preserve humor in English-to-Thai joke translation?
What are the most common humor loss patterns in machine-translated jokes?

methods_proposed.name: GPT-P
methods_proposed.summary: A humor-preserving prompt for GPT-4o that instructs the model to recognize humor such as wordplay, puns, and cultural references and adapt the translation naturally for Thai speakers while avoiding explanations.

methods_proposed.name: GPT-Ex
methods_proposed.summary: A humor-preserving GPT-4o prompt that additionally asks the model to provide an explanation in parentheses after the Thai translation, clarifying wordplay, double meanings, or cultural references when needed.

humor_theories.detail: The related work states that humor arises from unexpected contrasts in meaning and that humor translation relies on incongruity resolution, intention attribution, and cultural knowledge.
The related work mentions Superiority Theory as framing humor as deriving from perceived dominance over others.

The paper manually classifies jokes with a multi-label scheme—Wordplay, Idioms, Cultural Reference, Irony, Dark Humor, Absurd Humor, and Other—following prior work on linguistic vs. cultural humor in translation, taxonomies of linguistic jokes, and computational humor frameworks.

evaluation_methods.detail: Three Thai-native annotators manually categorized each translated joke as X (Not Understandable), XJ (Recognized as a Joke but Not Understood), J (Joke Understood), or N (Not a Joke); final labels were assigned by majority vote, with consensus discussion or blinded adjudication for 1–1–1 splits.

Humor retention was defined as the percentage of translations labeled J; the paper also reports full class distributions, exact pairwise McNemar tests on J vs. non-J, paired percentage-point gaps, win–loss ratios, shift heatmaps, and stratified retention by joke type.
Annotator reliability was reported as Fleiss’ Kappa κ = 0.6536 and simple agreement percentage 78.57% after written bilingual guidelines, oral explanations, and a 15-item test set.

headline_results.finding: GPT-Ex had the highest overall humor retention, followed by GPT-P, MT, and baseline GPT.
GPT-Ex significantly increased humor retention over MT.
Baseline GPT significantly decreased humor retention relative to MT.
GPT-P was numerically higher than MT, but the difference was not statistically significant at α = 0.05.
For wordplay, GPT-Ex showed a statistically robust improvement and the paper reports a large retention increase over baseline GPT.
headline_results.value: GPT 32.20% to GPT-Ex 59.81% (+27.61 percentage points); MT vs. GPT-Ex n01 = 113, n10 = 44, p = 3.5 × 10^{-8}

key_findings: Only GPT-Ex clearly outperformed MT overall: GPT-Ex achieved 62.94% retention versus MT’s 50.12%, while GPT-P reached 54.12% and baseline GPT only 41.06%.
Baseline GPT was significantly worse than MT, with ΔJ = −9.06 percentage points and exact McNemar p = 1.19 × 10^{-8}.
GPT-P improved numerically over MT by +4.00 percentage points, but this did not reach statistical significance at α = 0.05 (p = 0.0649).

The paper identifies two dominant baseline-GPT failure modes among jokes originally labeled J in MT: 45 cases (10.56%) became Not Understandable and 85 cases (19.95%) became Not a Joke, for 130 failed jokes (30.52%).

Stratified McNemar tests confirmed significant GPT-Ex gains over MT for Wordplay, Cultural Reference, Irony, and Dark Humor; Idioms, Absurd, and Other were not statistically significant because of small sample sizes.
The annotator protocol achieved Fleiss’κ = 0.6536 and 78.57% simple agreement, which the paper describes as moderate to substantial agreement and relatively high consistency.
limitations: The annotation protocol did not include an explicit complexity baseline, difficulty ratings, or calibrated easy/medium/hard exemplars.
Some joke-type categories had small sample sizes, especially Idioms (n = 24) and Absurd Humor (n = 28), limiting statistical power and making those results exploratory.
The study focuses on English-to-Thai joke translation, so the reported findings are tied to that language pair and its linguistic and cultural challenges.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes that some jokes in the Humorous Jokes Dataset may contain offensive content; the authors declare no conflict of interest.

artifacts.other: The data presented in this study are available on request from the corresponding author.
